grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: Rows show 25 °C, I=0.5 logK values for [M]+[L]<=>[ML]: 6.74 (Cl−), 9.07 (Br−), 12.87 (I−). The n=1 is implied by ML stoichiometry and not listed as a numeric “1” in the table.

paragraphs.text:
Notes:
- Trend: I− > Br− > Cl−, consistent with soft–soft Hg(II)–halide bonding. As I increases, higher‑halide anions (HgX3−, HgX42−) are favored.
- Thermodynamics (Br−, 0.5–3 M): exothermic with increasingly negative ΔH from n=1 to 4; ΔS positive (complexation driven by strong enthalpy and some entropy gain).

claims.text: As I increases, higher‑halide anions (HgX3−, HgX42−) are favored.
complexation driven by strong enthalpy and some entropy gain

Notes:
- Ordering at I≈0.1 M: TEDTA > EEDTA > EDTA > HEDTA. Significant complex stability; small decreases with higher ionic strength. Reported ΔH for these ML complexes is strongly exothermic with large positive ΔS, consistent with chelation.

paragraphs.text:
Notes:
- Only solid‑phase (sparingly soluble) mercurous halides were found here; no discrete aqueous Hg(I)–Xn− complexes in this set.
- Trend in Ksp magnitude: Cl < Br < I < CN (most negative logK, least soluble) aligns with increasing Hg–X “softness.” Calomel (Hg2Cl2) and related phases control Hg(I) speciation via solubility.

tool_snippets.snippet: Hg^[+] + Hydrogen cyanide — vlm_171967 | logK | -39.3 | 25 | 0 | `[M2L2(s)] <=> [M]^2 + [L]^2` | solid

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: Trend inferred from logK values at 25 °C: see rows for Cl (-17.91), Br (-22.25), I (-28.34), CN (-39.3) showing decreasing values Cl > Br > I > CN.
More negative logK for `[M2L2(s)] <=> M^2 + L^2` indicates a smaller dissolution constant (Ksp) and thus lower solubility; this is general solubility equilibrium knowledge.
HSAB principle: soft acid Hg(I) forms strongest interactions with softer anions (I−, CN−), aligning with lower solubility; not a database row value but accepted chemistry.

tool_snippets.snippet: Hg^[+] + Chloride ion — vlm_177467 | beta_def_458 | logK | -17.91 | 25 | 0 | `[M2L2(s)] <=> [M]^2 + [L]^2` | solid

paragraphs.text:
Big‑picture takeaways
- Hg(II) forms exceptionally strong complexes with soft anions: CN− >> I− > Br− > Cl−; thiosulfate and EDTA‑type chelators also bind strongly and can outcompete halides under matched conditions.
- Ionic strength matters: increasing I pushes equilibria toward higher‑halide anions (e.g., HgCl3−, HgCl42−), and modestly lowers chelate logβ1.
- Hg(I) exists predominantly as the dimer Hg2^2+ in water; halide chemistry is dominated by very low solubility of Hg2X2(s), not by well‑defined aqueous coordination series.

increasing I pushes equilibria toward higher‑halide anions
Hg(I) exists predominantly as the dimer Hg2^2+ in water
halide chemistry is dominated by very low solubility of Hg2X2(s)
not by well‑defined aqueous coordination series.
